role_summary: Mississippi-side civil rights movement organizer; her oral history clusters tightly with the Mississippi SNCC voter-registration cohort in this corpus's embedding.
ai_reading: Greene's top five semantic neighbors are all Mississippi SNCC voter-registration figures: Charles McLaurin (entry 17, count 154), Euvester Simpson (entry 41), Juadine Henderson (entry 68), Jennifer Lawson (entry 59), and Frankye Adams-Johnson (entry 43). The embedding therefore places her firmly inside the Mississippi voter-registration network, the same cluster that contains Simpson (whose testimony she most resembles) and McLaurin (whose Sunflower County project frames the cohort). Her position on the Southern Struggle vs Northern Struggle axis at +0.53 confirms the Mississippi geography. The catalog's strongest reading of her testimony is structural rather than biographical: she is one of the tightest single-cluster placements in the catalog so far, with no neighbors outside the Mississippi cohort in her top five and no axis position more than +0.53 in absolute value, which marks her in the embedding as a focused-network voice within the broader corpus.
biographical_paragraph: Freddie Greene (later Freddie Greene Biddle) was a Mississippi-side civil rights organizer; her oral history is held at the Library of Congress as entry 44 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1]. Independent biographical sourcing for Greene is pending; SNCC Digital Gateway and the Veterans of the Civil Rights Movement archive (crmvet.org) both returned 404 to the slug variants searched in this iteration. The Mississippi-SNCC cluster placement visible in her semantic neighbors above is the strongest signal currently available for her movement role.
movement_context: The Mississippi SNCC field-staff infrastructure that Greene's embedding cluster places her within was built between 1961 and 1965 as a project of the Council of Federated Organizations (COFO), the umbrella coalition Bob Moses, Aaron Henry, and David Dennis assembled in 1962 to coordinate SNCC, the Congress of Racial Equality (CORE), the NAACP, and the Southern Christian Leadership Conference work inside Mississippi under a single field-staff structure [src: 2]. COFO's voter-registration project ran from a network of county offices in McComb (Pike County), Greenwood (Leflore County), Hattiesburg (Forrest County), Indianola (Sunflower County), and Jackson (Hinds County), each staffed by SNCC and CORE field secretaries who lived in the communities they were organizing and worked through citizenship-school classes, mass meetings, and courthouse delegations rather than through episodic direct-action campaigns [src: 2]. The Charles M. Payne formulation of this approach as SNCC's "organizing tradition" (contrasted with the SCLC's "mobilizing tradition" of mass mobilization for set-piece campaigns) draws principally on the Sunflower County project Charles McLaurin (catalog entry 17) opened in August 1962 and on the Greenwood, McComb, and Hattiesburg circuits Euvester Simpson (catalog entry 41), Hollis Watkins, Curtis Hayes, Sam Block, and Willie Peacock worked through the same period [src: 3]. The 1964 Freedom Summer project COFO organized that summer drew approximately 700 to 1000 northern volunteers (largely white college students recruited through a campus broadside campaign coordinated from the New York and Atlanta SNCC offices) into the Mississippi field-staff network for a 10-week voter-registration and Freedom School effort; the volunteer recruitment was an explicit strategic decision Moses and the COFO staff made to force national press attention onto the violence Mississippi authorities directed at Black registration workers, on the analysis that the murders of white northerners would generate the federal response that the killings of Black Mississippians through 1961-1963 had not [src: 3, 4]. The June 21, 1964 disappearance and subsequent murder of CORE field secretary Michael Schwerner, Freedom Summer volunteer Andrew Goodman, and CORE Meridian organizer James Chaney near Philadelphia in Neshoba County (their bodies recovered August 4, 1964 from an earthen dam) made that analysis the documentary record [src: 4]. The post-1965 voter-registration continuation work the SNCC Mississippi field staff carried forward after the Voting Rights Act August 6, 1965 signing ran through the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party (MFDP), the Delta Ministry, and the Freedom Farm Cooperative network Fannie Lou Hamer organized from her Ruleville base; the field-staff cohort Greene's embedding cluster identifies as her movement context continued the registration, citizenship-school, and economic-organizing work through the late 1960s and into the 1970s as the official voter-registration ledgers in the Black-majority Delta counties moved from single-digit-percentage Black registration in 1962 to majority registration by the end of the decade [src: 5].
legacy_and_reception: Independent scholarly or institutional reception of Freddie Greene (Biddle) specifically is not established in the standard secondary literature on the Mississippi SNCC project as of this iteration of the catalog. Her Library of Congress oral history is the principal extant first-person record [src: 1]. The collective-memoir record of the SNCC Mississippi women's cohort in the Faith S. Holsaert, Martha Prescod Norman Noonan, Judy Richardson, Betty Garman Robinson, Jean Smith Young, and Dorothy M. Zellner edited 2010 University of Illinois Press volume Hands on the Freedom Plow: Personal Accounts by Women in SNCC includes accounts from approximately fifty SNCC women organizers across the McComb, Greenwood, Hattiesburg, Jackson, and Selma circuits; Greene's name does not appear in the front-matter table of contents, though the volume's coverage of the Mississippi cohort she organized within is the closest available institutional framing of her movement context [src: 6]. The SNCC Digital Gateway profile registry (the Duke University / SNCC Legacy Project collaborative archive working from the SNCC papers held at the Robert W. Woodruff Library at the Atlanta University Center) does not contain a person-page for Greene as of this iteration; the SNCC Legacy Project archive itself remains the primary institutional venue where additional first-person material on Greene's organizing work, if it exists, would surface [src: 7]. The conservative reading of the available record is that her oral history is the documentary anchor; further biographical work would require archival research at the Atlanta SNCC papers or direct interview with the SNCC Mississippi alumni network.

gallery.alt: SNCC Mississippi Freedom Summer Project recruitment broadside, April 1964
gallery.caption: The April 1964 SNCC recruitment broadside for the Mississippi Freedom Summer Project, calling for "1000 students" to join the COFO voter-registration and Freedom School effort that summer. The broadside was the principal campus-recruitment instrument for the approximately 700 to 1000 northern volunteers who arrived in Mississippi in June 1964 to work alongside the resident SNCC and CORE field-staff network that Greene's embedding cluster places her within; the Greenwood (Leflore County), McComb (Pike County), Hattiesburg (Forrest County), Indianola (Sunflower County), and Jackson (Hinds County) COFO offices were the principal field destinations the broadside directed volunteers toward.
gallery.photographer: Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (broadside design); scan held by Mississippi Department of Archives and History
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (via Mississippi Department of Archives and History)
gallery.license: Public domain (no known copyright restrictions; Mississippi Department of Archives and History)

gallery.alt: Sunflower County Courthouse in Indianola, Mississippi
gallery.caption: The Sunflower County Courthouse in Indianola, Mississippi. The COFO field-staff network Greene's embedding cluster identifies as her movement context ran courthouse-delegation voter-registration actions through this courthouse and the parallel county-seat courthouses in Greenwood, McComb, Hattiesburg, and Jackson; the August 31, 1962 SNCC action Charles McLaurin (catalog entry 17) led to this building opened the Sunflower County project and set the pattern of the registration confrontations that continued through Freedom Summer 1964 and into the post-Voting-Rights-Act registration continuation work.

gallery.alt: Tougaloo College Civil Rights Marker, Jackson, Mississippi
gallery.caption: The Mississippi Freedom Trail marker at Woodworth Chapel on the Tougaloo College campus in Jackson, Mississippi. The Tougaloo campus was the institutional anchor for the Jackson COFO office during Freedom Summer 1964 and through the Freedom Vote campaign, and the academic home Euvester Simpson (catalog entry 41) and other SNCC Mississippi field-staff veterans enrolled in after the Voting Rights Act passed in August 1965; the campus is one of the principal institutional sites in the SNCC Mississippi field-staff infrastructure Greene's embedding cluster places her within.

sources.title: Freddie Greene (Biddle) oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)

sources.title: John Dittmer, Local People: The Struggle for Civil Rights in Mississippi
sources.publisher: University of Illinois Press, 1994 (Bancroft Prize 1995); WorldCat OCLC 30816494 (the standard institutional history of the Mississippi civil rights movement, including the formation and field operation of the Council of Federated Organizations and the SNCC, CORE, NAACP, and SCLC coalition structure that staffed the McComb, Greenwood, Hattiesburg, Indianola, and Jackson voter-registration projects between 1961 and 1965)

sources.title: Charles M. Payne, I've Got the Light of Freedom: The Organizing Tradition and the Mississippi Freedom Struggle
sources.publisher: University of California Press, 1995 (second edition 2007); WorldCat OCLC 61664854 (the principal scholarly account of SNCC's Mississippi voter-registration project and the formulation of the "organizing tradition" framework that distinguishes the Mississippi field-staff method from the SCLC mobilizing-campaign approach)

sources.title: Bruce Watson, Freedom Summer: The Savage Season That Made Mississippi Burn and Made America a Democracy
sources.publisher: Viking, 2010 (paperback Penguin 2011); WorldCat OCLC 644565144 (the principal narrative history of the 1964 Freedom Summer project, including the campus volunteer recruitment, the COFO field-office network, and the Schwerner-Goodman-Chaney murders in Neshoba County)

sources.title: Kay Mills, This Little Light of Mine: The Life of Fannie Lou Hamer
sources.publisher: Plume / Penguin, 1994 (second edition University Press of Kentucky, 2007); WorldCat OCLC 29521716 (the standard biography of Hamer, documenting the post-1965 voter-registration continuation work through the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party, the Delta Ministry, and the Freedom Farm Cooperative network that the SNCC Mississippi field-staff cohort carried forward through the late 1960s and 1970s)

sources.title: Faith S. Holsaert, Martha Prescod Norman Noonan, Judy Richardson, Betty Garman Robinson, Jean Smith Young, and Dorothy M. Zellner, eds., Hands on the Freedom Plow: Personal Accounts by Women in SNCC
sources.publisher: University of Illinois Press, 2010 (paperback New Press 2014); WorldCat OCLC 635021942 (the principal collective-memoir archival document of the SNCC women's cohort, with accounts from approximately fifty SNCC women organizers covering the McComb, Greenwood, Hattiesburg, Jackson, and Selma circuits Greene's embedding cluster situates her within)

sources.title: SNCC Digital Gateway
sources.publisher: Duke University in collaboration with the SNCC Legacy Project (working from the SNCC papers held at the Robert W. Woodruff Library at the Atlanta University Center); the principal openly-accessible institutional archive of SNCC organizational history and personnel records

sources.title: Wesley C. Hogan, Many Minds, One Heart: SNCC's Dream for a New America
sources.publisher: University of North Carolina Press, 2007 (paperback 2009); WorldCat OCLC 192129687 (institutional history of SNCC documenting the Mississippi field-staff network, the 2014 SNCC 50th Anniversary Conference at Shaw University, and the SNCC Legacy Project archive at the Atlanta University Center)
